SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments are adequate in scope — they evaluate on the Yelp dataset, compare against linguistic and multiple behavioral baselines, and report Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy showing improvements (e.g., ~9.0% F1 gain in hotels). However, important analyses are missing: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not provided, and there is no evidence of ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., on the rating-constraint weight θ or the TransE vs. CNN contributions) to validate component contributions. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper discusses limitations and failure modes (cold-start weaknesses for behavioral features), but reproducibility and deeper ablation/sensitivity analysis are insufficient to fully support the claims. | B(3): The paper runs experiments on a realistic Yelp dataset and compares the joint model to linguistic, behavioral, and an independent supervised CNN baseline, showing clear gains (e.g., ~9% F1 in hotels), and discusses domain differences (hotel vs restaurant) and specific failure modes of behavioral features in cold-start. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters or code reported), and while the independent CNN comparison serves as a basic ablation, the summary does not indicate broader sensitivity analyses or hyperparameter sweeps for components like the rating-weight θ. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations are discussed, but the experimental analysis lacks some important ablations and reproducibility detail.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate in scope — they evaluate on the Yelp dataset, compare against linguistic and multiple behavioral baselines, and report Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy showing improvements (e.g., ~9.0% F1 gain in hotels). However, important analyses are missing: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not provided, and there is no evidence of ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., on the rating-constraint weight θ or the TransE vs. CNN contributions) to validate component contributions. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper discusses limitations and failure modes (cold-start weaknesses for behavioral features), but reproducibility and deeper ablation/sensitivity analysis are insufficient to fully support the claims.

However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters or code reported), and while the independent CNN comparison serves as a basic ablation, the summary does not indicate broader sensitivity analyses or hyperparameter sweeps for components like the rating-weight θ. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations are discussed, but the experimental analysis lacks some important ablations and reproducibility detail.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an NLP/ML problem (review spam detection) and proposes a joint neural modeling approach combining CNN-based text embeddings with a TransE-like behavioral embedding and rating constraints, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses standard ML/NLP evaluation practices (Yelp dataset, reasonable baselines including linguistic and behavioral features, and Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy metrics) and cites related work thoroughly, so the target audience would find it relevant. Minor scope concerns are that the evaluation is limited to a single dataset (Yelp), hyperparameter reporting is missing, and code availability is unclear — practical issues that warrant attention but do not preclude fit. | B(4): The paper addresses an NLP/ML problem (review spam detection in the cold-start setting) and proposes a representation-learning solution (joint CNN text embeddings + TransE-like behavioral embeddings) that aligns well with top-tier ML/NLP interests in combined text and graph/behavioral modeling. It evaluates on standard Yelp datasets against appropriate linguistic and behavioral baselines using common metrics (Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), and situates itself with thorough related work coverage. Minor scope concerns stem from being an applied domain-focused study (single dataset, hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), but the modeling and evaluation approach make it a good fit for these venues.

Minor scope concerns are that the evaluation is limited to a single dataset (Yelp), hyperparameter reporting is missing, and code availability is unclear — practical issues that warrant attention but do not preclude fit.

It evaluates on standard Yelp datasets against appropriate linguistic and behavioral baselines using common metrics (Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), and situates itself with thorough related work coverage. Minor scope concerns stem from being an applied domain-focused study (single dataset, hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), but the modeling and evaluation approach make it a good fit for these venues.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the practically important cold-start review-spam problem by proposing a concrete joint model that combines TransE-like (product, reviewer, review) behavioral embeddings with CNN-based text embeddings and rating-constraint supervision. Empirical gains on the Yelp dataset (e.g., ~9.0% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvement in the hotel domain) indicate the approach can materially improve detection in at least some settings. The modeling idea of jointly embedding global review-graph structure with text (and encoding rating polarity) is likely to influence subsequent work and practical systems, though the domain-dependent effectiveness (smaller gains when behavioral signals are sparse) limits it from being transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles an important and practical problem—review-spam detection in the cold-start setting—by proposing a joint embedding of textual (CNN) and global behavioral information (TransE-like review graph embeddings) plus rating constraints. The method yields substantial empirical gains on a real-world Yelp dataset (e.g., ~9.0% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvements in the hotel domain) over both text-only CNNs and traditional linguistic/behavioral baselines, indicating the approach could influence practice. By demonstrating how global reviewer/product structure can be integrated with text representations, the work is likely to spur further research on joint structural–text models for spam and related tasks. Its impact is somewhat tempered by results being reported on Yelp and by noted domain variability in behavioral signal strength, but overall it represents a strong contribution with clear applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the practically important cold-start review-spam problem by proposing a concrete joint model that combines TransE-like (product, reviewer, review) behavioral embeddings with CNN-based text embeddings and rating-constraint supervision. Empirical gains on the Yelp dataset (e.g., ~9.0% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvement in the hotel domain) indicate the approach can materially improve detection in at least some settings. The modeling idea of jointly embedding global review-graph structure with text (and encoding rating polarity) is likely to influence subsequent work and practical systems, though the domain-dependent effectiveness (smaller gains when behavioral signals are sparse) limits it from being transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important and practical problem—review-spam detection in the cold-start setting—by proposing a joint embedding of textual (CNN) and global behavioral information (TransE-like review graph embeddings) plus rating constraints. The method yields substantial empirical gains on a real-world Yelp dataset (e.g., ~9.0% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvements in the hotel domain) over both text-only CNNs and traditional linguistic/behavioral baselines, indicating the approach could influence practice. By demonstrating how global reviewer/product structure can be integrated with text representations, the work is likely to spur further research on joint structural–text models for spam and related tasks. Its impact is somewhat tempered by results being reported on Yelp and by noted domain variability in behavioral signal strength, but overall it represents a strong contribution with clear applicability.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (joint CNN text embeddings with TransE-style behavioral embeddings and rating constraints) are stated early and described clearly through method and experiments. Notation and methodological description appear consistent enough to follow (margin-based ranking loss, θ hyperparameter), and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation has minor gaps impacting clarity and reproducibility: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and the effectiveness of figures/tables is unclear from the summary. Overall writing is adequate but would benefit from these additional presentation details. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a logical flow from problem to method to experiments, and its main contributions (joint CNN text embeddings with TransE-style behavioral embeddings and a rating-constraint) are stated early. Notation and the core methodology are generally clear—e.g., the margin-based ranking loss and CNN structure are described—and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation issues are minor: hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned, which harms reproducibility, and writing is characterized as adequate rather than exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (joint CNN text embeddings with TransE-style behavioral embeddings and rating constraints) are stated early and described clearly through method and experiments. Notation and methodological description appear consistent enough to follow (margin-based ranking loss, θ hyperparameter), and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation has minor gaps impacting clarity and reproducibility: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and the effectiveness of figures/tables is unclear from the summary. Overall writing is adequate but would benefit from these additional presentation details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a logical flow from problem to method to experiments, and its main contributions (joint CNN text embeddings with TransE-style behavioral embeddings and a rating-constraint) are stated early. Notation and the core methodology are generally clear—e.g., the margin-based ranking loss and CNN structure are described—and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation issues are minor: hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned, which harms reproducibility, and writing is characterized as adequate rather than exceptional.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clear new modeling perspective—jointly learning CNN-based text embeddings with TransE-style reviewer/product/review behavioral embeddings and explicit rating constraints—to tackle cold-start review spam, which is not a standard prior approach. The summary shows the method is differentiated from independent CNN and traditional behavioral features and applies a non-trivial combination of knowledge-graph-style embeddings and sentiment/rating constraints. While the components (CNN, TransE, margin losses) are known, their joint application to cold-start spam detection and the rating-constraint formulation represent a notable, not merely trivial, extension that would be informative to experts. | B(4): The paper proposes a clear new perspective by jointly embedding review text (via a CNN) and global behavioral structure (via a TransE-like embedding of (product, reviewer, review) triples) and further incorporating rating embeddings as constraints—an approach not reported in the provided summary. It explicitly contrasts this joint model with an independent supervised CNN and traditional behavioral/linguistic baselines on the Yelp cold-start setting, so contributions are clearly differentiated. The method combines established components in a non-trivial way tailored to cold-start review spam detection, yielding notable novelty though built from existing techniques rather than a radical conceptual leap.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clear new modeling perspective—jointly learning CNN-based text embeddings with TransE-style reviewer/product/review behavioral embeddings and explicit rating constraints—to tackle cold-start review spam, which is not a standard prior approach. The summary shows the method is differentiated from independent CNN and traditional behavioral features and applies a non-trivial combination of knowledge-graph-style embeddings and sentiment/rating constraints. While the components (CNN, TransE, margin losses) are known, their joint application to cold-start spam detection and the rating-constraint formulation represent a notable, not merely trivial, extension that would be informative to experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clear new perspective by jointly embedding review text (via a CNN) and global behavioral structure (via a TransE-like embedding of (product, reviewer, review) triples) and further incorporating rating embeddings as constraints—an approach not reported in the provided summary. It explicitly contrasts this joint model with an independent supervised CNN and traditional behavioral/linguistic baselines on the Yelp cold-start setting, so contributions are clearly differentiated. The method combines established components in a non-trivial way tailored to cold-start review spam detection, yielding notable novelty though built from existing techniques rather than a radical conceptual leap.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s central claims are clearly stated and are supported by experiments showing measurable gains (e.g., ~9% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvement in the hotel domain) and by a plausible technical approach (joint CNN text embeddings with a TransE-style behavioral embedding plus rating constraints). Baselines and evaluation metrics are appropriate (independent CNN, linguistic/behavioral baselines, Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), so the experimental methodology appears sound in design. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and there is no mention of statistical significance testing; these are minor but nontrivial shortcomings that do not, in my judgement, overturn the main technical conclusions. | B(4): The paper’s main claim—a joint CNN text embedding plus TransE-style behavioral embedding with rating constraints improves cold-start review spam detection—is clearly stated and supported by experiments reporting ~9% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy gains over strong baselines (independent CNN, linguistic and behavioral features). The proposed losses and model components are technically coherent (standard CNN + margin-based TransE-like loss), so no glaring mathematical errors are apparent from the summary. However, important methodological details that affect reproducibility and confidence are missing in the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests or released code), which are minor but nontrivial issues. Overall the evidence supports the core claims but with some gaps in experimental reporting.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s central claims are clearly stated and are supported by experiments showing measurable gains (e.g., ~9% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvement in the hotel domain) and by a plausible technical approach (joint CNN text embeddings with a TransE-style behavioral embedding plus rating constraints). Baselines and evaluation metrics are appropriate (independent CNN, linguistic/behavioral baselines, Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), so the experimental methodology appears sound in design. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and there is no mention of statistical significance testing; these are minor but nontrivial shortcomings that do not, in my judgement, overturn the main technical conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claim—a joint CNN text embedding plus TransE-style behavioral embedding with rating constraints improves cold-start review spam detection—is clearly stated and supported by experiments reporting ~9% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy gains over strong baselines (independent CNN, linguistic and behavioral features). The proposed losses and model components are technically coherent (standard CNN + margin-based TransE-like loss), so no glaring mathematical errors are apparent from the summary. However, important methodological details that affect reproducibility and confidence are missing in the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests or released code), which are minor but nontrivial issues. Overall the evidence supports the core claims but with some gaps in experimental reporting.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a reasonable set of baselines (linguistic bigrams, traditional behavioral features RL/RD/MCS, independent supervised CNN, SVM combos, and behavioral approximations like BF EditSim/BF W2V), and uses the same Yelp dataset and standard metrics (Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), so comparisons are largely appropriate and fair. The independent CNN baseline uses the same architecture without behavioral info, which supports a fair ablation. The paper also analyzes when behavioral features help versus hurt (hotel vs restaurant domains) and discusses limitations. Minor weaknesses are that hyperparameters and code are not reported (limiting full reproducibility/inspection of fairness) and it is not fully clear from the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods match or outperform their model. | B(4): The paper compares against a diverse set of appropriate baselines (linguistic bigrams, traditional behavioral features RL/RD/MCS, independent CNN, SVM combinations, and behavioral approximations like BF EditSim+LF and BF W2Vsim+W2V) using the same Yelp dataset and standard metrics (precision/recall/F1/accuracy). It also discusses limitations and acknowledges cases where behavioral features provide smaller gains or decrease performance (restaurant vs. hotel domains), indicating awareness when prior approaches perform comparably. A minor omission is lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of more recent neural baselines, which prevents a fully comprehensive assessment of fairness and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable set of baselines (linguistic bigrams, traditional behavioral features RL/RD/MCS, independent supervised CNN, SVM combos, and behavioral approximations like BF EditSim/BF W2V), and uses the same Yelp dataset and standard metrics (Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), so comparisons are largely appropriate and fair. The independent CNN baseline uses the same architecture without behavioral info, which supports a fair ablation. The paper also analyzes when behavioral features help versus hurt (hotel vs restaurant domains) and discusses limitations. Minor weaknesses are that hyperparameters and code are not reported (limiting full reproducibility/inspection of fairness) and it is not fully clear from the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods match or outperform their model.

It also discusses limitations and acknowledges cases where behavioral features provide smaller gains or decrease performance (restaurant vs. hotel domains), indicating awareness when prior approaches perform comparably. A minor omission is lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of more recent neural baselines, which prevents a fully comprehensive assessment of fairness and reproducibility.

main_claims: Proposes a novel neural network model to detect review spam in the cold-start setting by jointly embedding textual and behavioral information.
Encodes global behavioral information from the review graph via a TransE-like embedding of (product, reviewer, review) triples and jointly learns review text embeddings with a CNN.
Incorporates rating embeddings (1–5 stars) as constraints during joint learning to capture sentiment polarity in reviews.

Experimental results on a Yelp dataset show the proposed joint model outperforms an independent supervised CNN and traditional linguistic/behavioral baselines (e.g., ~9.0% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvement in the hotel domain).
_summary.methodology: Jointly learn review text embeddings via a CNN and behavioral/product/reviewer embeddings via a TransE-style margin-based ranking loss; combine this structural loss with a rating-constraint loss (weighted by hyperparameter θ) to produce review representations that encode both textual and global behavioral information.
datasets: Yelp dataset (as used in Mukherjee et al., 2013b,c; Rayana and Akoglu, 2015)
baselines: Traditional behavioral features: Review Length (RL), Reviewer Deviation (RD), Maximum Content Similarity (MCS)
Independent supervised CNN (same CNN structure without review-graph/behavioral info)
SVM classifiers using combinations of the above features
BF EditSim+LF (behavioral features approximated via edit-distance similar existing reviews)
BF W2Vsim+W2V (behavioral features approximated via Word2Vec similarity)
limitations: Traditional statistical behavioral features are ineffective in the cold-start setting due to lack of behavioral history.
Using the three traditional behavioral features (RL, RD, MCS) in the cold-start setting caused decreases in F1 and Recall and increased false positives.

Behavioral features only yield clear improvements when abundant behavioral information is available (e.g., hotel domain), and provide much smaller gains when few follow-up reviews exist (e.g., restaurant domain).
The CNN (text model) tends to perform better at identifying real reviews than at identifying spam (higher accuracy than F1).
